---
title: "Adaptive and Modern Optimizers: AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the Optimization track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE   = "#2b3a67"    # plain SGD / baseline path
STEP    = "#c1121f"    # the adaptive path / the interesting curve
ACCENT  = "#0a7d4d"    # optimum / Adam / safe choices
GREY    = "#6c757d"
EXTRA   = "#d97706"    # a third method (RMSProp / AdaGrad)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the **terminal practical node** of the
> Optimization track's descent spine — the article that explains what practitioners
> *actually* type when they train a model. It assumes [OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*] for the
> bare update $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}=\mathbf{x}_k-\eta\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ and the
> steepest-descent reading of $-\nabla f$; [OPTM-07 *Step Size and Convergence*] for
> **$L$-smoothness**, the **condition number** $\kappa=L/\mu$, and the fact that GD's speed
> is throttled by $\kappa$; [OPTM-08 *Momentum*] for the **velocity** term
> $\mathbf{v}_{k+1}=\beta\mathbf{v}_k-\eta\nabla f$ as an exponentially weighted memory of
> past gradients; and [OPTM-11 *Stochastic Gradient Descent*] for the **stochastic
> gradient** $\nabla f_{i_k}$, its **unbiasedness**, its non-vanishing **variance**
> $\sigma^2$, the **noise floor**, and the decaying-step machinery.
>
> **What it borrows, and from where.** Nothing new from outside the track. Every object —
> gradients, exponential moving averages, the steepest-descent argument, the condition
> number — has already been built in OPTM-06 through OPTM-11. This article *assembles* them
> into the three optimizers the field runs on, deriving each update term by term.
>
> **What it sets up.** This is the engine room. **Classification**, **Unsupervised
> Learning**, and every deep-model treatment that says "trained with Adam" or "fit by SGD
> with momentum" links here for what those words mean. It is the last descent-spine article;
> after it, the track turns to constrained optimization (OPTM-13 onward).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Plain stochastic gradient descent ([OPTM-11]) uses **one scalar step size** $\eta$ for
every parameter. That is a problem the moment different parameters live on different scales
or receive gradients of wildly different magnitudes — a rare, high-information feature and a
common, low-information one cannot share a learning rate without one of them being
mistuned. This article derives the family of **adaptive** optimizers that fix this by giving
*each coordinate its own effective step size*, computed on the fly from the gradients that
coordinate has seen. We build them in the order history did. **AdaGrad** accumulates the
*sum* of squared gradients per coordinate and divides the step by its square root — derived
from a regret-minimization argument and motivated by sparse features — then we expose its
fatal flaw: the accumulator only grows, so the effective step decays to zero and learning
halts. **RMSProp** replaces the ever-growing sum with an **exponential moving average**
(the same fading memory as momentum, [OPTM-08]), so the scale tracks *recent* curvature and
never starves. **Adam** fuses the two ideas the track has built — momentum on the gradient
(a first-moment EMA) and RMSProp's per-coordinate scaling (a second-moment EMA) — and adds
a **bias-correction** step that we derive exactly, fixing the cold-start undercounting of an
EMA initialized at zero. We close at the honest edge: Adam's fast early progress versus its
documented tendency to generalize *worse* than well-tuned SGD-with-momentum, the
convergence counterexample that forced AMSGrad, the decoupled-weight-decay fix (AdamW), and
the practitioner's actual decision rule for which optimizer to reach for.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Plain SGD scales *every* coordinate of the gradient by the same step size $\eta$. **Adaptive
methods give each coordinate its own step size, shrinking the step for coordinates whose
gradients have been large and keeping it big for coordinates whose gradients have been
small.** Track a per-coordinate running measure $v_j$ of the squared gradient in coordinate
$j$, and step
$$
x_{j,\,k+1} = x_{j,k} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{v_{j,k}}+\varepsilon}\,g_{j,k},
$$
so the *effective* learning rate in coordinate $j$ is $\eta/\sqrt{v_{j,k}}$. The three
methods differ only in how they form $v_j$. **AdaGrad** uses the *cumulative sum*
$v_{j,k}=\sum_{t\le k} g_{j,t}^2$ — which adapts to sparse, badly-scaled features but
**grows without bound**, so every effective step decays to zero and training stalls.
**RMSProp** replaces the sum with an *exponential moving average*
$v_{j,k}=\rho v_{j,k-1}+(1-\rho)g_{j,k}^2$, a fading memory of *recent* squared gradients
that never starves. **Adam** combines RMSProp's second-moment EMA with a *first-moment* EMA
of the gradient itself — momentum ([OPTM-08]) — and **bias-corrects** both moving averages
to undo the zero-initialization undercount. The result is the default optimizer of modern
machine learning: robust to scaling, fast out of the gate, and forgiving of a poorly chosen
learning rate. The honest catch, which the article ends on: Adam's speed sometimes comes at
the cost of *worse generalization* than carefully tuned SGD-with-momentum, and a naive
moving average can even break convergence — fixes the field had to bolt on afterward.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The single shared move — *divide each coordinate's step by
the square root of a running average of its squared gradient* — is the whole idea. AdaGrad,
RMSProp, and Adam are three answers to one sub-question: *which* running average? Read the
article as the evolution of that one choice, each version fixing the last one's specific,
nameable failure.

# Why we are here: one step size cannot fit every parameter {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **why is a single global learning rate the
wrong tool the moment parameters differ in scale or activity, and what is the minimal change
to the SGD step that fixes it?** Every derivation answers a piece of that.

## The wound SGD left open {#sec-wound}

[OPTM-07] diagnosed gradient descent's central weakness — it is throttled by the **condition
number** $\kappa=L/\mu$, the ratio of the steepest to the gentlest curvature. In an
ill-conditioned bowl the negative gradient points mostly *across* the narrow valley rather
than *along* it, and a single step size $\eta$ must be small enough not to diverge in the
steep direction, which makes it agonizingly slow in the gentle direction. [OPTM-08]'s
momentum attacked this by *remembering* the consistent downhill direction. But momentum
still applies the **same** step size to every coordinate. The deeper disease is untreated:
**different coordinates genuinely want different step sizes.**

Make that concrete. Suppose the objective, near the optimum, looks like a quadratic with
axis-aligned curvatures that differ by orders of magnitude — coordinate $1$ steep, coordinate
$2$ flat:

$$
f(x_1, x_2) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(L\,x_1^2 + \mu\,x_2^2\bigr),
\qquad L \gg \mu.
$$

The gradient is $\nabla f = (L x_1,\ \mu x_2)$. A *single* $\eta$ faces an impossible
compromise: to keep the steep coordinate from diverging it must obey $\eta < 2/L$, but then
the flat coordinate moves by only $\eta\mu \ll 1$ of the way each step and crawls. The
*right* answer is obvious if you are allowed to use it: **a different step size per
coordinate** — large for the flat coordinate, small for the steep one. That is exactly what a
second-order method ([OPTM-09]) computes from the Hessian, paying $O(p^3)$ for the privilege.
The adaptive optimizers of this article are the cheap, first-order imitation: *estimate how
much each coordinate's gradient has been bouncing, and shrink the step where it bounces
hardest* — all from the gradients you already compute, at $O(p)$ cost.

```{python}
#| label: fig-why-anisotropic
#| fig-cap: 'Why one step size cannot win. The objective $f=\tfrac12(L x_1^2+\mu x_2^2)$ with $L=30$, $\mu=1$ -- steep in $x_1$, flat in $x_2$. **Navy:** plain GD with the largest stable step ($\eta$ just under $2/L$): it is safe in the steep $x_1$ direction but barely moves along the flat $x_2$ direction, so it crawls down the valley floor. **Red:** the *same* step rescaled *per coordinate* by $1/\sqrt{\text{curvature}}$ -- the adaptive idea -- which races to the optimum because each coordinate finally gets the step it wants. The grey arrows on the floor show the per-coordinate step the navy path is forced to share.'
L, mu = 30.0, 1.0
def grad(x): return np.array([L * x[0], mu * x[1]])

x0 = np.array([1.0, 4.0])
# Plain GD: largest stable step
eta = 1.9 / L
xg = x0.copy(); pathG = [xg.copy()]
for _ in range(60):
    xg = xg - eta * grad(xg); pathG.append(xg.copy())
pathG = np.array(pathG)

# Per-coordinate (idealized adaptive) step: divide each coord by sqrt(curvature)
scale = np.array([np.sqrt(L), np.sqrt(mu)])
xa = x0.copy(); pathA = [xa.copy()]
eta_a = 0.25
for _ in range(60):
    xa = xa - eta_a * grad(xa) / scale**2 * scale  # = eta_a * grad / scale
    pathA.append(xa.copy())
pathA = np.array(pathA)

gx = np.linspace(-1.2, 1.2, 200); gy = np.linspace(-1.0, 4.5, 200)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy)
Z = 0.5 * (L * GX**2 + mu * GY**2)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.6))
ax.contour(GX, GY, Z, levels=np.linspace(1, 250, 22), colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.7)
ax.plot(pathG[:, 0], pathG[:, 1], "-o", color=CURVE, ms=3, lw=1.4, label="plain GD (one step size)")
ax.plot(pathA[:, 0], pathA[:, 1], "-o", color=STEP, ms=3, lw=1.6, label="per-coordinate step (adaptive idea)")
ax.scatter([x0[0]], [x0[1]], color="black", s=55, zorder=6, label="start")
ax.scatter([0], [0], color=ACCENT, s=110, zorder=6, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.4, label="minimum")
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$ (steep)"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$ (flat)")
ax.set_title("One step size compromises; per-coordinate steps do not")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The sparse-feature problem that birthed AdaGrad {#sec-sparse}

There is a second, equally common situation where one step size fails, and it is the one
AdaGrad was explicitly designed for. In many real models — text classification with
bag-of-words features, recommendation systems, click prediction — the input features are
**sparse**: most are zero in any given example. A feature like "contains the word
*defenestration*" is nonzero in a handful of documents and zero in millions of others.

Now think about what a per-example gradient does to that feature's weight. The gradient of
the loss with respect to a feature's weight is *zero whenever the feature is zero in the
current example* (the weight does not enter the prediction, so it cannot affect the loss).
So a rare feature's weight receives a gradient signal only on the rare steps where the
feature fires — say once in $10{,}000$ steps. A common feature's weight is updated on
essentially every step. Under a single global $\eta$, the rare feature's weight is updated
$10{,}000$ times less often, and effectively never learns, even though those rare features
are often the *most informative* ones (a rare word can be a near-perfect class signal).

The fix AdaGrad proposes is intuitive once stated: **give each coordinate a step size
inversely related to how much total gradient signal it has accumulated.** A rare feature,
having accumulated little, keeps a large step and takes a big stride on the rare occasions it
fires; a common feature, having accumulated a lot, takes small, careful steps. We derive this
precisely in @sec-adagrad. Hold onto the picture: *adaptivity is per-coordinate fairness — it
lets the infrequent, high-information coordinates keep up with the frequent, low-information
ones.*

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just normalize the features so they are all on the same scale, and use plain
SGD?"* You can and should standardize *dense* features, and it genuinely helps with the
anisotropy of @sec-wound. But it does not solve the sparse-feature problem of @sec-sparse:
standardizing changes a feature's *magnitude*, not its *frequency*. A rare standardized
feature still fires rarely, so its weight still receives a gradient on only a few steps, and
a global step size still cannot serve it and the common features at once. Adaptivity adjusts
to *update frequency and history*, which preprocessing cannot. The honest summary: feature
scaling and adaptive optimizers attack overlapping but distinct problems — scaling fixes
static differences in magnitude, adaptivity fixes dynamic differences in how often and how
hard each coordinate is pushed. In practice deep nets use both (normalization layers *and*
Adam), and an interviewer is checking that you see they are not redundant.
:::

## A two-line history: from online convex optimization to every transformer {#sec-history}

The lineage is tight and recent. **AdaGrad** came from **Duchi, Hazan, and Singer** (2011),
out of the *online convex optimization* / regret-minimization community — its per-coordinate
$1/\sqrt{\text{accumulated gradient}}$ scaling falls out of minimizing a regret bound, which
we sketch in @sec-adagrad. Its decaying-rate flaw was felt almost immediately. **RMSProp**
was proposed by **Geoffrey Hinton** in 2012 — famously, in a *Coursera lecture slide*, never
formally published — as the one-line fix: swap AdaGrad's cumulative sum for an exponential
moving average. **Adam** (**Kingma and Ba**, 2014) combined RMSProp's per-coordinate scaling
with classical momentum ([OPTM-08]) and added bias correction; its name is short for
"*adaptive moment estimation*." Within a few years Adam was the default optimizer for nearly
every deep network, and it remains the workhorse for training large language models today.
Holding this history in view explains the article's shape: each method is a *named patch* on
the previous one's *named flaw*, and you understand the family best by understanding the
sequence of repairs, not by memorizing three update rules.

# Intuition before symbols: per-coordinate terrain sensing {#sec-intuition}

Before any formulas, here is the mental image that makes all three methods one idea. Return to
the blindfolded walker of [OPTM-06], descending a hillside whose shape differs along different
compass directions: maybe steep and rutted east–west, smooth and gently sloping north–south.
Plain SGD forces the walker to use the *same stride length* in every direction. To avoid
stumbling in the rutted east–west direction they must take tiny, cautious steps — and then
they barely make progress in the smooth north–south direction where long strides would be
perfectly safe.

An adaptive walker does something smarter: **they sense the terrain separately in each
direction and adjust their stride per direction.** Where the ground has been bucking and
heaving (large, erratic gradients), they shorten their stride for safety. Where it has been
smooth (small, steady gradients), they lengthen it to cover ground. They estimate "how rough
has it been lately" in each direction from the only information they have — the magnitudes of
the slopes they have recently felt — and set each direction's stride to roughly
$\eta/\sqrt{\text{recent roughness}}$.

That single sentence is AdaGrad, RMSProp, and Adam. The three methods are three answers to
*"how do you measure 'recent roughness'?"* AdaGrad sums up *all* the roughness ever felt —
which means the longer the walk, the more cautious every stride becomes, until the walker is
taking infinitesimal steps and effectively stops. RMSProp measures roughness with a *fading
memory*, weighting recent terrain more than ancient terrain, so the stride tracks the
*current* ground and never seizes up. Adam adds the momentum idea from [OPTM-08] on top —
the walker also remembers the *consistent direction* of the slope, not just its roughness —
so they build speed along reliable downhill directions while still shortening stride over
rough ground. @fig-roadmap previews the payoff on the anisotropic bowl.

```{python}
#| label: fig-roadmap
#| fig-cap: 'The whole article on one slide. The anisotropic bowl $f=\tfrac12(L x_1^2+\mu x_2^2)$, $L=30$, $\mu=1$, all methods from the same start with a *single global* base learning rate. **Plain SGD-style GD** (navy) is forced into a tiny safe step and crawls. **AdaGrad** (orange) rescales per coordinate and accelerates -- but watch it slow late as its accumulator grows. **RMSProp** (red) keeps a healthy per-coordinate step throughout. **Adam** (green) adds momentum and sweeps to the optimum fastest. Same gradients, same base $\eta$; the only difference is how each method sets its *per-coordinate* effective step.'
L, mu = 30.0, 1.0
def grad(x): return np.array([L * x[0], mu * x[1]])
x0 = np.array([1.0, 4.0]); eps = 1e-8; base_eta = 0.30; nsteps = 80

def run_gd(eta=1.9/L):
    x = x0.copy(); P = [x.copy()]
    for _ in range(nsteps):
        x = x - eta * grad(x); P.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(P)
def run_adagrad(eta=base_eta):
    x = x0.copy(); acc = np.zeros(2); P = [x.copy()]
    for _ in range(nsteps):
        g = grad(x); acc += g**2
        x = x - eta * g / (np.sqrt(acc) + eps); P.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(P)
def run_rmsprop(eta=base_eta, rho=0.9):
    x = x0.copy(); v = np.zeros(2); P = [x.copy()]
    for _ in range(nsteps):
        g = grad(x); v = rho * v + (1 - rho) * g**2
        x = x - eta * g / (np.sqrt(v) + eps); P.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(P)
def run_adam(eta=base_eta, b1=0.9, b2=0.999):
    x = x0.copy(); m = np.zeros(2); v = np.zeros(2); P = [x.copy()]
    for t in range(1, nsteps + 1):
        g = grad(x); m = b1 * m + (1 - b1) * g; v = b2 * v + (1 - b2) * g**2
        mh = m / (1 - b1**t); vh = v / (1 - b2**t)
        x = x - eta * mh / (np.sqrt(vh) + eps); P.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(P)

gx = np.linspace(-1.2, 1.2, 200); gy = np.linspace(-1.0, 4.6, 200)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy); Z = 0.5 * (L * GX**2 + mu * GY**2)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.8))
ax.contour(GX, GY, Z, levels=np.linspace(1, 250, 22), colors=GREY, linewidths=0.6, alpha=0.6)
for P, col, lab in [(run_gd(), CURVE, "plain GD"),
                    (run_adagrad(), EXTRA, "AdaGrad"),
                    (run_rmsprop(), STEP, "RMSProp"),
                    (run_adam(), ACCENT, "Adam")]:
    ax.plot(P[:, 0], P[:, 1], "-", lw=1.7, color=col, label=lab)
ax.scatter([x0[0]], [x0[1]], color="black", s=50, zorder=6, label="start")
ax.scatter([0], [0], color=ACCENT, s=100, zorder=6, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.3)
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$ (steep)"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$ (flat)")
ax.set_title("Four optimizers, same start, same base $\\eta$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Formal development I: AdaGrad {#sec-adagrad}

We now make "sense the terrain per coordinate" precise, starting with the first and simplest
adaptive method. Throughout, $\mathbf{g}_k$ denotes the gradient at step $k$ — the *stochastic*
gradient $\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k)$ in the SGD setting of [OPTM-11], or the full gradient
when the data are small; the adaptive machinery is identical either way. We write its $j$-th
coordinate as $g_{j,k}$, the $j$-th coordinate of the parameter as $x_{j,k}$, and we work
coordinate by coordinate because that is the entire point.

## The update rule {#sec-adagrad-rule}

::: {#def-adagrad}
**Definition (AdaGrad).** Maintain a per-coordinate accumulator $G_{j,k}$ of squared
gradients, initialized $G_{j,0}=0$. At each step, for every coordinate $j$,
$$
G_{j,k} = G_{j,k-1} + g_{j,k}^2,
\qquad
x_{j,\,k+1} = x_{j,k} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{j,k}} + \varepsilon}\,g_{j,k},
$$ {#eq-adagrad}
where $\eta>0$ is a global base step size and $\varepsilon>0$ is a tiny constant (typically
$10^{-8}$) present only to prevent division by zero before any gradient has accumulated.
:::

Unpack @eq-adagrad against the Core. The accumulator $G_{j,k}=\sum_{t=1}^{k}g_{j,t}^2$ is the
**cumulative sum of squared gradients** in coordinate $j$ — a running tally of how much that
coordinate has been pushed, in total, up to now. The *effective* step size in coordinate $j$
is

$$
\eta_{j,k}^{\text{eff}} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{j,k}}+\varepsilon}
= \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\sum_{t=1}^{k} g_{j,t}^2}+\varepsilon}.
$$

Read it: a coordinate that has accumulated large squared gradients gets a *small* effective
step; one that has accumulated little keeps a *large* one. This is exactly the per-coordinate
fairness of @sec-sparse — a rare feature, having fired seldom, has a small $G_j$ and so takes
a big stride when it finally does fire — and the per-coordinate rescaling of @sec-wound, with
the steep coordinate (large gradients) automatically slowed.

Why the *square root*, and not, say, dividing by $G_{j,k}$ itself? Two readings, both worth
carrying. **Dimensionally:** $g_{j,k}^2$ has the units of (gradient)$^2$, so
$\sqrt{G_{j,k}}$ has the units of gradient, and $g_{j,k}/\sqrt{G_{j,k}}$ is *dimensionless* —
the update is then $\eta$ times a unitless quantity, so $\eta$ carries the units of the
parameter and the rescaling is scale-free. Dividing by $G_{j,k}$ instead would leave an
inverse-gradient unit and make the method behave erratically under rescaling. **Statistically:**
$\sqrt{G_{j,k}/k}$ is (up to the mean) the *root-mean-square* gradient magnitude in coordinate
$j$, so the step divides the gradient by an estimate of its own typical size — the natural,
scale-invariant thing to do. The square root will reappear, for the same reasons, in RMSProp
and Adam.

## Where the $1/\sqrt{\cdot}$ comes from: the regret argument {#sec-adagrad-regret}

The square-root rule is not an arbitrary choice that happens to work — it is the rule that
*minimizes a worst-case regret bound*, and the derivation is illuminating enough to give in
outline (the full bound lives in Duchi–Hazan–Singer 2011; we build the load-bearing step). In
online convex optimization you face a sequence of convex losses $f_1, f_2, \dots$, one per
step, and after seeing each you update $\mathbf{x}$. **Regret** measures how much worse your
running choices did than the single best fixed point in hindsight:

$$
R_K = \sum_{k=1}^{K} f_k(\mathbf{x}_k) - \min_{\mathbf{x}}\sum_{k=1}^{K} f_k(\mathbf{x}).
$$

A standard analysis of a per-coordinate step size $\eta_{j}$ (held fixed for the bound) gives
a regret contribution from coordinate $j$ of the form

$$
R_{K}^{(j)} \;\le\; \frac{D_j^2}{2\eta_j} \;+\; \frac{\eta_j}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K} g_{j,k}^2,
$$ {#eq-regret}

where $D_j$ bounds how far the iterate can roam in coordinate $j$. The first term is the cost
of starting far from the optimum (large when the step is small — you cannot get there); the
second is the cost of the gradients buffeting you (large when the step is big — you overreact).
We minimize the bound over $\eta_j$. Differentiate the right-hand side of @eq-regret with
respect to $\eta_j$ and set it to zero (the Calc-1 move, exactly OPTM-01):

$$
\frac{d}{d\eta_j}\left[\frac{D_j^2}{2\eta_j} + \frac{\eta_j}{2}\sum_k g_{j,k}^2\right]
= -\frac{D_j^2}{2\eta_j^2} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_k g_{j,k}^2 = 0
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\eta_j^\star = \frac{D_j}{\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{K} g_{j,k}^2}}.
$$

There it is: **the regret-optimal per-coordinate step size is proportional to
$1/\sqrt{\sum_k g_{j,k}^2}$** — one over the square root of the accumulated squared gradient,
exactly AdaGrad's effective step. The cumulative sum and the square root are not heuristics;
they are the closed-form minimizer of the worst-case regret. Substituting $\eta_j^\star$ back
into @eq-regret collapses the two terms into a single $D_j\sqrt{\sum_k g_{j,k}^2}$, and summing
over coordinates yields AdaGrad's regret bound — provably no worse, and often much better, than
the best fixed global step in hindsight, especially when the gradients are sparse (most
$g_{j,k}=0$, so the sums stay small).

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what does AdaGrad's adaptivity buy you that a tuned constant step does
not?"* It automatically allocates a *larger* total learning rate to coordinates that receive
*less* total gradient signal — and the regret bound proves this allocation is optimal in
hindsight, with the biggest gains exactly when the gradients are sparse or scaled very
differently across coordinates. A tuned constant step is optimal only if you happen to pick
the single $\eta$ that is best *on average* across all coordinates, which is necessarily a
compromise; AdaGrad gets the right $\eta$ per coordinate, for free, from the gradient history.
:::

## The fatal flaw: the accumulator only grows {#sec-adagrad-flaw}

Now the flaw the Core promised, and it is baked into @eq-adagrad. The accumulator
$G_{j,k}=\sum_{t=1}^k g_{j,t}^2$ is a sum of *non-negative* terms, so it is **monotonically
non-decreasing**: it never shrinks, and as long as the coordinate keeps receiving any gradient
signal at all, it grows without bound, $G_{j,k}\to\infty$. The effective step
$\eta/\sqrt{G_{j,k}}$ therefore **decays to zero** as training proceeds — and it does so
regardless of whether the iterate has actually reached the optimum.

This is exactly right for a *convex, one-pass* online problem — the regret derivation wanted
the step to shrink like $1/\sqrt{k}$, the Robbins–Monro-flavored decay of [OPTM-11]. But it is
*wrong* for the long, multi-epoch training of a deep model, where you want to keep learning at
a healthy rate for a long time. There, AdaGrad's monotone decay is premature: the effective
step can shrink to near-zero while the model is still far from a good solution, and training
**stalls** — not because it converged, but because every coordinate's step starved. The very
mechanism that gives AdaGrad its clean regret bound (an accumulator that only grows) is what
kills it on the problems we care most about.

@fig-adagrad-decay makes the starvation visible: AdaGrad's effective step size sliding
inexorably toward zero, and the resulting loss curve flattening far above the optimum.

```{python}
#| label: fig-adagrad-decay
#| fig-cap: 'AdaGrad starves itself. A logistic-regression-style smooth convex objective trained for many steps. **Left:** AdaGrad''s effective step size $\eta/\sqrt{G_k}$ for a representative coordinate, on a log scale -- it decays monotonically toward zero because the accumulator $G_k$ only ever grows, never forgetting old gradients. **Right:** the resulting suboptimality. AdaGrad (orange) makes fast early progress, then **stalls** as its step starves -- the curve flattens far above the optimum -- while RMSProp (red), which forgets old gradients via an EMA (next section), keeps a healthy step and continues descending. The flaw is structural, not a tuning mistake.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n, p = 600, 25
X = rng.standard_normal((n, p))
wtrue = rng.standard_normal(p)
ylog = (X @ wtrue + 0.5 * rng.standard_normal(n) > 0).astype(float)
def sig(z): return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-z))
def floss(w):
    z = X @ w
    return np.mean(np.logaddexp(0, z) - ylog * z)
def fgrad(w): return X.T @ (sig(X @ w) - ylog) / n
# good reference optimum via many GD steps
wref = np.zeros(p)
for _ in range(4000): wref = wref - 1.0 * fgrad(wref)
fstar = floss(wref)
eps = 1e-8; eta = 0.5

# AdaGrad
w = np.zeros(p); G = np.zeros(p); ada_loss = []; ada_eff = []
for k in range(1, 4001):
    g = fgrad(w); G += g**2
    w = w - eta * g / (np.sqrt(G) + eps)
    if k % 20 == 0:
        ada_loss.append(floss(w) - fstar); ada_eff.append(eta / (np.sqrt(G[0]) + eps))
# RMSProp
w = np.zeros(p); v = np.zeros(p); rms_loss = []
for k in range(1, 4001):
    g = fgrad(w); v = 0.9 * v + 0.1 * g**2
    w = w - 0.05 * g / (np.sqrt(v) + eps)
    if k % 20 == 0: rms_loss.append(floss(w) - fstar)
ks = np.arange(20, 4001, 20)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.0))
axL.semilogy(ks, ada_eff, color=EXTRA)
axL.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); axL.set_ylabel("AdaGrad effective step (log)")
axL.set_title("Effective step starves to zero")
axR.semilogy(ks, np.clip(ada_loss, 1e-10, None), color=EXTRA, label="AdaGrad")
axR.semilogy(ks, np.clip(rms_loss, 1e-10, None), color=STEP, label="RMSProp")
axR.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$f-f^\star$ (log)")
axR.set_title("AdaGrad stalls; RMSProp keeps going")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Formal development II: RMSProp {#sec-rmsprop}

The fix for AdaGrad's starvation is to make the accumulator **forget**. That is precisely
what an exponential moving average does, and we have already built one: it is the same fading
memory as momentum's velocity ([OPTM-08]).

## From a growing sum to a fading average {#sec-rmsprop-rule}

The problem was that $G_{j,k}=\sum_{t\le k} g_{j,t}^2$ weights every past squared gradient
*equally and forever*. RMSProp replaces it with a weighted average that *discounts* the past
geometrically, so a squared gradient from a thousand steps ago has negligible influence.

::: {#def-rmsprop}
**Definition (RMSProp).** Maintain a per-coordinate **exponential moving average (EMA)** of
squared gradients, $v_{j,0}=0$. With a decay rate $\rho\in[0,1)$ (typically $0.9$ or $0.99$),
$$
v_{j,k} = \rho\,v_{j,k-1} + (1-\rho)\,g_{j,k}^2,
\qquad
x_{j,\,k+1} = x_{j,k} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{v_{j,k}}+\varepsilon}\,g_{j,k}.
$$ {#eq-rmsprop}
:::

The step is *identical* to AdaGrad's @eq-adagrad except that the cumulative sum $G_{j,k}$ is
replaced by the EMA $v_{j,k}$. To see that this is a genuine fading memory — and to make the
"$\sqrt{v}$ is a running RMS" reading exact — unroll the recurrence exactly as [OPTM-08]
unrolled the velocity. With $v_{j,0}=0$,

$$
v_{j,k} = (1-\rho)\sum_{t=1}^{k} \rho^{\,k-t}\,g_{j,t}^2.
$$ {#eq-rmsprop-unroll}

So $v_{j,k}$ is a **weighted average of past squared gradients**, with the most recent
($t=k$) carrying weight $(1-\rho)$, the one before it $(1-\rho)\rho$, then $(1-\rho)\rho^2$,
and so on — weights that decay geometrically into the past and (as we verify next) sum to one
in the limit, making $v_{j,k}$ a true *average*, the running mean-square gradient. The
contrast with AdaGrad is the whole point: AdaGrad's weights are all $1$ (nothing forgotten);
RMSProp's decay, so the average tracks *recent* gradient magnitude. The name is literal: the
step divides by $\sqrt{v}$, the **r**oot-**m**ean-**s**quare of recent gradients —
**RMSProp**.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does forgetting fix the starvation, mechanically?"* Because the EMA reaches a *steady
state* instead of growing without bound. If a coordinate's squared gradients hover around
some level $s$, then $G_k=\sum g_t^2 \approx ks$ grows linearly forever (AdaGrad), so
$\eta/\sqrt{G_k}\approx \eta/\sqrt{ks}\to 0$. But the EMA settles: plug a constant $g_t^2=s$
into @eq-rmsprop-unroll and the geometric sum gives $v_k\to s$, a *constant*, so the effective
step $\eta/\sqrt{v_k}\to \eta/\sqrt{s}$ stabilizes at a finite, healthy value and stays there.
Forgetting converts a divergent sum into a convergent average — that is the entire repair.
:::

## Verifying the EMA is a true average {#sec-rmsprop-weights}

We claimed the weights $(1-\rho)\rho^{\,k-t}$ sum to (essentially) one, so $v_{j,k}$ really
is a weighted *average* and not just a weighted sum. Sum the weights in @eq-rmsprop-unroll
over the $k$ past terms. Let $s = k-t$ run from $0$ (the present) to $k-1$ (the oldest):

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{k} (1-\rho)\rho^{\,k-t}
= (1-\rho)\sum_{s=0}^{k-1}\rho^{\,s}
= (1-\rho)\cdot\frac{1-\rho^{\,k}}{1-\rho}
= 1 - \rho^{\,k}.
$$

The middle step is the finite geometric-series formula $\sum_{s=0}^{k-1}\rho^s =
\frac{1-\rho^k}{1-\rho}$ (a Calc-precursor identity: multiply the sum by $(1-\rho)$ and watch
all but the first and last terms telescope). So the weights sum to $1-\rho^k$, which is
**slightly less than $1$ for small $k$** and rises to $1$ as $k\to\infty$ (since $\rho^k\to0$
for $|\rho|<1$). For large $k$ the EMA is a true average. The deficit $\rho^k$ at small $k$ is
not a rounding nuisance — it is a genuine bias, the EMA *undercounts* early on because it is
averaging against a phantom zero (the initialization $v_0=0$). That undercount is harmless
enough in RMSProp that it is usually ignored, but it is the exact defect Adam will
**bias-correct** in @sec-adam-bias. Notice it now; we cash it out shortly.

# Formal development III: Adam {#sec-adam}

Adam — *adaptive moment estimation* — is the synthesis the whole track has been building
toward. It layers the two ideas we have in hand: **momentum** ([OPTM-08]) on the gradient
itself, and **RMSProp**'s per-coordinate scaling by recent squared-gradient magnitude. Then
it fixes the small-$k$ undercount of @sec-rmsprop-weights with an exact bias correction. We
build it term by term.

## The two moments {#sec-adam-moments}

Adam maintains *two* per-coordinate EMAs. The first is an EMA of the gradient — the
"velocity" of [OPTM-08], here called the **first moment** because it estimates the *mean* of
the gradient:

$$
m_{j,k} = \beta_1\,m_{j,k-1} + (1-\beta_1)\,g_{j,k},
\qquad m_{j,0}=0.
$$ {#eq-adam-m}

The second is RMSProp's EMA of the squared gradient — the **second (raw) moment**, estimating
the *mean square* of the gradient:

$$
v_{j,k} = \beta_2\,v_{j,k-1} + (1-\beta_2)\,g_{j,k}^2,
\qquad v_{j,0}=0.
$$ {#eq-adam-v}

The decay rates are conventionally $\beta_1=0.9$ (the gradient memory, the same role as
momentum's $\beta$) and $\beta_2=0.999$ (a *longer* memory for the squared-gradient scale, so
the per-coordinate step is steady). The names "first/second moment" are exact: $m$ is a
running estimate of $\mathbb{E}[g_j]$ and $v$ of $\mathbb{E}[g_j^2]$, the first two moments of
the (noisy, stochastic) gradient in coordinate $j$. Adam *estimates the mean gradient and the
mean squared gradient, per coordinate, online.*

## Why these two, together {#sec-adam-why}

Hold the two moments side by side and read what each contributes — this is the design logic,
and it ties straight back to the *why*. The first moment $m_{j,k}$ is **momentum**: it
accumulates the gradient's *consistent direction*, averaging out the stochastic noise of
[OPTM-11] and the zig-zag of [OPTM-08], so the step points along the reliable downhill trend
rather than the latest noisy sample. The second moment $v_{j,k}$ is **RMSProp's terrain
sensing**: $\sqrt{v_{j,k}}$ is the recent RMS gradient magnitude in coordinate $j$, so
dividing by it gives the per-coordinate rescaling that fixes anisotropy (@sec-wound) and
sparse-feature fairness (@sec-sparse).

The combined step, ignoring bias correction for one moment, is

$$
x_{j,\,k+1} = x_{j,k} - \eta\,\frac{m_{j,k}}{\sqrt{v_{j,k}}+\varepsilon},
$$

which reads beautifully: *step in the direction of the smoothed (momentum) gradient, with a
per-coordinate stride inversely proportional to that coordinate's recent gradient
magnitude.* The numerator decides **direction and consistency**; the denominator decides
**per-coordinate scale**. Momentum without scaling (plain [OPTM-08]) still suffers anisotropy;
scaling without momentum (RMSProp) still chases noise; Adam does both. There is even a clean
"signal-to-noise" reading: when the gradient is consistent, $|m_j|$ is large and close to
$\sqrt{v_j}$, so the ratio is near $\pm1$ and Adam takes a confident unit-ish step; when the
gradient is pure noise that averages to zero, $m_j\to0$ while $\sqrt{v_j}$ stays positive, so
the ratio shrinks and Adam takes a cautious small step. The update *automatically slows down
exactly where the gradient signal is unreliable.*

## Bias correction, derived exactly {#sec-adam-bias}

Now the piece that distinguishes Adam from "RMSProp plus momentum," and the one most worth
deriving in full because it is the most commonly hand-waved. Both EMAs start at zero,
$m_0=v_0=0$, so in the early steps they are biased *toward zero* — they have not had time to
"warm up" to the true moment, exactly the $1-\rho^k$ deficit we flagged in
@sec-rmsprop-weights. Adam corrects this *exactly*, and here is the derivation.

Take the second moment $v_{j,k}$; the first is identical with $\beta_1$ in place of $\beta_2$.
From the unrolled form (same algebra as @eq-rmsprop-unroll, with $\beta_2$ for $\rho$),

$$
v_{j,k} = (1-\beta_2)\sum_{t=1}^{k}\beta_2^{\,k-t}\,g_{j,t}^2.
$$

We want to know the **expected value** of $v_{j,k}$ and compare it to the quantity it is meant
to estimate, $\mathbb{E}[g_{j,k}^2]$. Take expectations of both sides. To make the dependence
clean, make the standard mild assumption that the second moment is roughly stationary over the
averaging window — $\mathbb{E}[g_{j,t}^2]\approx\mathbb{E}[g_{j,k}^2]$ for the $t$ that carry
non-negligible weight (this is exact if the second moment is constant, and a good
approximation otherwise; any residual is folded into a small error term). Then
$\mathbb{E}[g_{j,t}^2]$ pulls out of the sum as a common factor:

$$
\mathbb{E}[v_{j,k}]
= (1-\beta_2)\sum_{t=1}^{k}\beta_2^{\,k-t}\,\mathbb{E}[g_{j,t}^2]
\approx \mathbb{E}[g_{j,k}^2]\cdot(1-\beta_2)\sum_{t=1}^{k}\beta_2^{\,k-t}.
$$

The remaining sum is exactly the geometric sum we computed in @sec-rmsprop-weights, equal to
$1-\beta_2^{\,k}$. Therefore

$$
\mathbb{E}[v_{j,k}] \approx \bigl(1-\beta_2^{\,k}\bigr)\,\mathbb{E}[g_{j,k}^2].
$$ {#eq-bias}

Read @eq-bias: the EMA's expectation is the true second moment **multiplied by the shrink
factor $1-\beta_2^{\,k}$**, which is small for small $k$ (e.g. with $\beta_2=0.999$ and $k=1$,
the factor is $0.001$ — the estimate is a thousand times *too small*) and rises to $1$ as
$k\to\infty$. The EMA systematically *undercounts* the true moment early on, by precisely the
factor $1-\beta_2^k$. The fix is now forced: **divide by the shrink factor to undo it.** Define
the **bias-corrected** moments

$$
\hat m_{j,k} = \frac{m_{j,k}}{1-\beta_1^{\,k}},
\qquad
\hat v_{j,k} = \frac{v_{j,k}}{1-\beta_2^{\,k}}.
$$ {#eq-adam-hat}

By @eq-bias, $\mathbb{E}[\hat v_{j,k}]\approx\mathbb{E}[g_{j,k}^2]$ — the correction makes the
estimate **unbiased**, exactly canceling the $1-\beta_2^k$ deficit. (The first moment is
identical with $\beta_1$.) For large $k$ the correction factor $\to1$ and does nothing; it
matters *only* in the early steps, where without it Adam's denominator $\sqrt{v}$ would be
absurdly small and the very first updates would explode. Bias correction is what lets Adam use
an aggressive base $\eta$ from step one without blowing up.

::: {#def-adam}
**Definition (Adam).** With $\beta_1,\beta_2\in[0,1)$, base step $\eta$, and $\varepsilon>0$,
initialize $m_0=v_0=0$ and at each step $k=1,2,\dots$, per coordinate, form the two moment
EMAs, bias-correct them, and step:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{j,k} &= \beta_1 m_{j,k-1} + (1-\beta_1)g_{j,k}, &
v_{j,k} &= \beta_2 v_{j,k-1} + (1-\beta_2)g_{j,k}^2, \\[4pt]
\hat m_{j,k} &= \frac{m_{j,k}}{1-\beta_1^{\,k}}, &
\hat v_{j,k} &= \frac{v_{j,k}}{1-\beta_2^{\,k}},
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
x_{j,\,k+1} = x_{j,k} - \eta\,\frac{\hat m_{j,k}}{\sqrt{\hat v_{j,k}}+\varepsilon}.
$$ {#eq-adam}
The standard defaults are $\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.999$, $\varepsilon=10^{-8}$, and $\eta$
around $10^{-3}$ — and they work so robustly across problems that Adam is often run with no
tuning at all beyond $\eta$.
:::

@fig-bias makes the correction's effect visible: without it, the first dozen Adam steps are
violently mis-scaled; with it, they are calibrated from step one.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bias
#| fig-cap: 'Bias correction, made visible. **Left:** the shrink factor $1-\beta_2^k$ (red) for $\beta_2=0.999$ and $1-\beta_1^k$ (navy) for $\beta_1=0.9$. The first-moment factor warms up in ~30 steps; the second-moment factor takes thousands -- so early on the *uncorrected* $v$ is drastically too small. **Right:** Adam on a simple quadratic, with and without bias correction. *Without* correction (orange) the first steps are wildly oversized -- $\sqrt{v}$ is near zero, so $m/\sqrt{v}$ blows up -- and the path lurches before recovering. *With* correction (green) every step is properly scaled from the start. The fix matters precisely in the opening steps.'
b1, b2 = 0.9, 0.999
ks = np.arange(1, 200)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.0))
axL.plot(ks, 1 - b1**ks, color=CURVE, label=r"$1-\beta_1^k$ ($\beta_1=0.9$)")
axL.plot(ks, 1 - b2**ks, color=STEP, label=r"$1-\beta_2^k$ ($\beta_2=0.999$)")
axL.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); axL.set_ylabel("EMA weight sum (warm-up factor)")
axL.set_title("How fast each EMA warms up")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

# Adam with / without bias correction on a simple quadratic
def quad_grad(x): return np.array([3.0 * x[0], 1.0 * x[1]])
def run_adam_bias(correct, eta=0.1, steps=60):
    x = np.array([2.0, 2.0]); m = np.zeros(2); v = np.zeros(2); eps = 1e-8; P = [x.copy()]
    for t in range(1, steps + 1):
        g = quad_grad(x)
        m = b1 * m + (1 - b1) * g; v = b2 * v + (1 - b2) * g**2
        if correct:
            mh = m / (1 - b1**t); vh = v / (1 - b2**t)
        else:
            mh, vh = m, v
        x = x - eta * mh / (np.sqrt(vh) + eps); P.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(P)
Pno = run_adam_bias(False); Pyes = run_adam_bias(True)
gx = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 150); gy = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 150)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy); Z = 0.5 * (3 * GX**2 + GY**2)
axR.contour(GX, GY, Z, levels=14, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.6, alpha=0.6)
axR.plot(Pno[:, 0], Pno[:, 1], "-o", ms=2.5, color=EXTRA, label="no bias correction")
axR.plot(Pyes[:, 0], Pyes[:, 1], "-o", ms=2.5, color=ACCENT, label="with bias correction")
axR.scatter([0], [0], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=6, edgecolor="white")
axR.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); axR.set_title("Early steps: corrected vs. not")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run all four optimizers on real, honest problems — never a toy rigged to flatter
adaptivity. The first is the regime adaptivity was built for; the second is a real,
representative deep-style problem; the third is the edge of applicability, where adaptivity
*loses*.

## Example 1 — sparse text-style features: where AdaGrad shines {#sec-ex-sparse}

This is the problem of @sec-sparse made concrete: logistic regression on **sparse** features,
where most coordinates are zero in any given example and a few rare coordinates carry strong
signal. This is the habitat AdaGrad was designed for, and the example is honest because it is
exactly the bag-of-words / click-prediction setting where adaptive methods first won.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-sparse
#| fig-cap: "Sparse features: adaptivity earns its keep. Logistic regression on a synthetic-but-realistic sparse dataset ($n=2000$, $p=200$, ~95% of feature entries zero, with a few rare-but-informative coordinates). All methods use a *single global* base learning rate. **Plain SGD** (navy) is forced to a small global step and cannot give the rare, informative coordinates the large strides they need, so it learns them painfully slowly. **AdaGrad** (orange), **RMSProp** (red), and **Adam** (green) all give those rare coordinates per-coordinate large steps and reach a good solution far faster. This is the regime where the adaptive idea is unambiguously the right tool."
rng = np.random.default_rng(8)
n, p = 2000, 200
# sparse design: each entry nonzero with prob 0.05
mask = rng.random((n, p)) < 0.05
X = mask * rng.standard_normal((n, p))
wtrue = np.zeros(p); idx = rng.choice(p, 15, replace=False)
wtrue[idx] = rng.standard_normal(15) * 2.5
y = (X @ wtrue + 0.5 * rng.standard_normal(n) > 0).astype(float)
def sig(z): return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-z))
def floss(w):
    z = X @ w; return np.mean(np.logaddexp(0, z) - y * z)
def gi(w, i): return (sig(X[i] @ w) - y[i]) * X[i]
wref = np.zeros(p)
for _ in range(2000): wref = wref - 0.5 * (X.T @ (sig(X @ wref) - y) / n)
fstar = floss(wref); eps = 1e-8

def train(method, steps=8000, eta=0.05):
    w = np.zeros(p); m = np.zeros(p); v = np.zeros(p); G = np.zeros(p); hist = []
    for k in range(1, steps + 1):
        i = rng.integers(n); g = gi(w, i)
        if method == "sgd":
            w = w - eta * g
        elif method == "adagrad":
            G += g**2; w = w - eta * g / (np.sqrt(G) + eps)
        elif method == "rmsprop":
            v = 0.9 * v + 0.1 * g**2; w = w - eta * g / (np.sqrt(v) + eps)
        elif method == "adam":
            m = 0.9 * m + 0.1 * g; v = 0.999 * v + 0.001 * g**2
            mh = m / (1 - 0.9**k); vh = v / (1 - 0.999**k)
            w = w - eta * mh / (np.sqrt(vh) + eps)
        if k % 40 == 0: hist.append(floss(w) - fstar)
    return np.array(hist)

ks = np.arange(40, 8001, 40)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
for meth, col, lab in [("sgd", CURVE, "plain SGD"), ("adagrad", EXTRA, "AdaGrad"),
                       ("rmsprop", STEP, "RMSProp"), ("adam", ACCENT, "Adam")]:
    ax.semilogy(ks, np.clip(train(meth), 1e-8, None), color=col, label=lab)
ax.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$f-f^\star$ (log)")
ax.set_title("Sparse features: adaptive methods beat plain SGD")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The story is clean: on genuinely sparse data with a shared global step, plain SGD cannot serve
the rare informative coordinates, and all three adaptive methods leave it behind. Note AdaGrad
does *well here* — this short, near-convex run is exactly the online setting its regret bound
targets, and its monotone decay has not yet had time to starve it. That is the honest other
half of @sec-adagrad-flaw: AdaGrad's flaw bites on long, non-convex training, not on the short
convex problems it was built for.

## Example 2 — a small neural network: the modern default {#sec-ex-nn}

The second example is the one practitioners actually face: training a small non-convex model
(a two-layer neural network on a nonlinear classification task). There is no closed form, the
landscape is non-convex with the saddles of [OPTM-02], and this is the regime where Adam
became the default. We train the same network with each optimizer and watch the training loss.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-nn
#| fig-cap: 'The modern default, on a non-convex problem. A two-layer tanh network ($2\to16\to1$) trained on a two-moons-style nonlinear classification task, minibatch size 32, each optimizer with a *reasonable* (lightly tuned) learning rate. **Plain SGD** (navy) makes slow, noisy progress and is sensitive to the step size. **RMSProp** (red) and especially **Adam** (green) descend faster and more smoothly with far less tuning -- the per-coordinate scaling tames the wildly different gradient magnitudes across the network''s weights and biases. AdaGrad (orange) starts well but its monotone decay (@sec-adagrad-flaw) slows it late. This robustness-with-little-tuning is exactly why Adam became the default for deep models.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
# two-moons-ish data
nA = 400
th = rng.uniform(0, np.pi, nA)
Xa = np.c_[np.cos(th), np.sin(th)] + 0.12 * rng.standard_normal((nA, 2))
Xb = np.c_[1 - np.cos(th), 0.5 - np.sin(th)] + 0.12 * rng.standard_normal((nA, 2))
Xd = np.vstack([Xa, Xb]); yd = np.r_[np.zeros(nA), np.ones(nA)]
N = Xd.shape[0]; H = 16

def init():
    return {"W1": rng.standard_normal((2, H)) * 0.5, "b1": np.zeros(H),
            "W2": rng.standard_normal((H, 1)) * 0.5, "b2": np.zeros(1)}
def fwd(P, X):
    h = np.tanh(X @ P["W1"] + P["b1"]); z = h @ P["W2"] + P["b2"]
    return z.ravel(), h
def loss_grad(P, X, y):
    z, h = fwd(P, X); s = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))
    L = np.mean(np.logaddexp(0, z) - y * z)
    dz = (s - y) / len(y)
    gW2 = h.T @ dz[:, None]; gb2 = np.array([dz.sum()])
    dh = dz[:, None] @ P["W2"].T * (1 - h**2)
    gW1 = X.T @ dh; gb1 = dh.sum(axis=0)
    return L, {"W1": gW1, "b1": gb1, "W2": gW2, "b2": gb2}
def full_loss(P): return loss_grad(P, Xd, yd)[0]

keys = ["W1", "b1", "W2", "b2"]; eps = 1e-8
def train_nn(method, steps=3000, eta=0.05, bs=32):
    P = init()
    m = {k: np.zeros_like(P[k]) for k in keys}; v = {k: np.zeros_like(P[k]) for k in keys}
    G = {k: np.zeros_like(P[k]) for k in keys}; hist = []
    for k in range(1, steps + 1):
        idx = rng.integers(0, N, bs)
        _, g = loss_grad(P, Xd[idx], yd[idx])
        for key in keys:
            gg = g[key]
            if method == "sgd":
                P[key] -= eta * gg
            elif method == "adagrad":
                G[key] += gg**2; P[key] -= eta * gg / (np.sqrt(G[key]) + eps)
            elif method == "rmsprop":
                v[key] = 0.9 * v[key] + 0.1 * gg**2
                P[key] -= eta * gg / (np.sqrt(v[key]) + eps)
            elif method == "adam":
                m[key] = 0.9 * m[key] + 0.1 * gg; v[key] = 0.999 * v[key] + 0.001 * gg**2
                mh = m[key] / (1 - 0.9**k); vh = v[key] / (1 - 0.999**k)
                P[key] -= eta * mh / (np.sqrt(vh) + eps)
        if k % 20 == 0: hist.append(full_loss(P))
    return np.array(hist)

ks = np.arange(20, 3001, 20)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
for meth, col, lab, e in [("sgd", CURVE, "plain SGD", 0.2), ("adagrad", EXTRA, "AdaGrad", 0.1),
                          ("rmsprop", STEP, "RMSProp", 0.01), ("adam", ACCENT, "Adam", 0.02)]:
    ax.plot(ks, train_nn(meth, eta=e), color=col, label=f"{lab}")
ax.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); ax.set_ylabel("training loss")
ax.set_title("Two-layer network: Adam/RMSProp descend fastest with little tuning")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 3 — the edge: where well-tuned SGD wins on generalization {#sec-ex-edge}

Now the honest edge, the example that separates "knows the update rules" from "knows when not
to use them." Adam's fast *training*-loss descent does not always translate into the best
*test*-set performance. There is substantial evidence — and we reproduce its mechanism — that
on some problems Adam converges to solutions that **generalize worse** than those found by
well-tuned plain SGD or SGD-with-momentum, even though Adam's training loss is just as low.

The cleanest way to *see* this is the **overparametrized** regime, which is where modern deep
models live: more parameters than training examples, so the model has the capacity to fit the
training data *exactly* — and crucially, it can do so in *many different ways*. When a whole
family of weight vectors drives the training loss to zero, the training loss alone can no
longer choose between them; the *optimizer* does, through its **implicit bias** — the
particular zero-loss solution its update rule happens to drift toward. Plain SGD and
SGD-with-momentum are biased toward small-norm, simple solutions (a fact tied to their
uniform per-coordinate step); Adam's per-coordinate rescaling biases it toward a *different*
interpolating solution, and on noisy data that solution tends to fit the noise more and
generalize worse. So we make the gap visible by plotting **both** losses side by side: the
training loss (where the two optimizers tie) and the *held-out* test loss (where they separate).

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-edge
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability: same training fit, worse generalization. An **overparametrized** noisy regression ($n_{\text{train}}=60$ examples, $p=200$ weights, so the model has more than enough capacity to fit the training data exactly). **Left -- training loss:** both optimizers drive it to essentially zero; **Adam** (green) gets there faster. There is *nothing left to separate them on the data you fit.* **Right -- held-out test loss:** here they part ways. **SGD with momentum** (navy) settles at a clearly *lower* test loss; **Adam** plateaus *higher*. Both interpolate the training set, but they reach *different* interpolating solutions, and Adam''s per-coordinate rescaling steers it to one that fits the noise more and generalizes worse (its final weight norm is larger). This is the documented Adam-vs-SGD generalization gap (Wilson et al. 2017): when many solutions fit the data, the optimizer''s implicit bias decides which one you land on -- and Adam''s is often the worse one for test performance.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
ntr, nte, p = 60, 4000, 200          # overparametrized: p >> n_train, many interpolating solutions
Xtr = rng.standard_normal((ntr, p)); Xte = rng.standard_normal((nte, p))
wtrue = np.zeros(p); wtrue[:5] = rng.standard_normal(5) * 1.5   # a few strong signal coords
ytr = Xtr @ wtrue + 0.3 * rng.standard_normal(ntr)
yte = Xte @ wtrue + 0.3 * rng.standard_normal(nte)
def tr_loss(w): return 0.5 * np.mean((Xtr @ w - ytr)**2)
def te_loss(w): return 0.5 * np.mean((Xte @ w - yte)**2)
eps = 1e-8

def run(method, steps=8000, eta=0.01, b1m=0.9, bs=10):
    w = np.zeros(p); m = np.zeros(p); v = np.zeros(p); tr = []; te = []
    for k in range(1, steps + 1):
        idx = rng.integers(0, ntr, bs); r = Xtr[idx] @ w - ytr[idx]; g = Xtr[idx].T @ r / bs
        if method == "sgdm":
            m = b1m * m - eta * g; w = w + m            # heavy-ball form of OPTM-08
        elif method == "adam":
            m = 0.9 * m + 0.1 * g; v = 0.999 * v + 0.001 * g**2
            mh = m / (1 - 0.9**k); vh = v / (1 - 0.999**k)
            w = w - eta * mh / (np.sqrt(vh) + eps)
        if k % 40 == 0: tr.append(tr_loss(w)); te.append(te_loss(w))
    return np.array(tr), np.array(te), np.linalg.norm(w)

ks = np.arange(40, 8001, 40)
tr_a, te_a, nrm_a = run("adam", eta=0.01)
tr_s, te_s, nrm_s = run("sgdm", eta=0.01)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.0))
axL.semilogy(ks, np.clip(tr_a, 1e-6, None), color=ACCENT, label="Adam")
axL.semilogy(ks, np.clip(tr_s, 1e-6, None), color=CURVE, label="SGD + momentum")
axL.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); axL.set_ylabel("training loss (log)")
axL.set_title("Training loss: both fit, Adam faster")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
axR.plot(ks, te_a, color=ACCENT, label=f"Adam ($\\|w\\|={nrm_a:.1f}$)")
axR.plot(ks, te_s, color=CURVE, label=f"SGD + momentum ($\\|w\\|={nrm_s:.1f}$)")
axR.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); axR.set_ylabel("held-out test loss")
axR.set_title("Test loss: SGD+momentum generalizes better")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Lower training loss is not lower test loss.** Adam's headline strength — driving the
*training* objective down fast — is measured on the data you fit, and that is not what you
ultimately care about. On problems with limited data or heavy noise, Adam's aggressive
per-coordinate adaptivity can fit the training noise more eagerly than SGD, landing in a
"sharper" minimum that generalizes worse. The fix in practice is not to abandon Adam but to
*regularize it properly* (weight decay, and specifically **AdamW**, @sec-amsgrad-adamw) and to
*always evaluate on held-out data*, never on the training loss alone. If you tune a learning
rate by watching training loss go down, you are optimizing the wrong quantity — the universal
version of this article's edge case.
:::

# Failure modes and the honest debate {#sec-failure}

## When SGD-with-momentum still wins {#sec-sgdm}

The generalization gap of @sec-ex-edge is the headline caveat, and it has a concrete decision
rule. For problems where you have the time and compute to **tune carefully**, where the
dataset is large enough that overfitting is the main risk, and where **final test performance
is the metric** — large-scale image classification is the classic example — well-tuned **SGD
with momentum** ([OPTM-08]) frequently matches or beats Adam on the test set, and many
state-of-the-art vision results are trained that way. Adam's advantage is **robustness and
speed with little tuning**: it gets a *good* model fast, almost regardless of the learning
rate, which is exactly what you want for rapid iteration, for problems where you cannot afford
a learning-rate sweep, and for architectures (transformers, RNNs) where the gradient scales
across layers are so heterogeneous that per-coordinate adaptivity is nearly mandatory. The
honest one-liner: *Adam to get something working fast and for heterogeneous-scale models;
tuned SGD+momentum to squeeze out the last drop of generalization when you can afford it.*

## The convergence counterexample, and the fixes (AMSGrad, AdamW) {#sec-amsgrad-adamw}

There is a deeper, theoretical failure worth knowing. In 2018, Reddi, Kale, and Kumar
(*"On the Convergence of Adam and Beyond"*) exhibited a simple convex problem on which Adam
**provably fails to converge** to the optimum — it cycles. The mechanism is the EMA's
*forgetting*: the second moment $v_k$ can *decrease* from one step to the next (an EMA, unlike
AdaGrad's monotone sum, can shrink when recent gradients are small), which can cause the
effective step to *grow* at the wrong moment and let a rare but informative large gradient be
washed out by the forgetting before it has been acted on. The proposed fix, **AMSGrad**, is
small and pointed: use the *running maximum* of the second moment, $\hat v_k^{\max}=\max(\hat
v_{k-1}^{\max},\hat v_k)$, in the denominator, so the effective step is *non-increasing* and
the pathology cannot occur. AMSGrad restores the convergence guarantee at a small practical
cost; in practice plain Adam usually converges fine, but knowing *why* it can fail — and that
the fix is "stop the denominator from shrinking" — is exactly the kind of thing an interviewer
probes.

A second, more practically important fix concerns **weight decay**. The standard way to
regularize is to add an $\ell_2$ penalty $\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\|\mathbf{x}\|^2$ to the
objective, whose gradient is $\lambda\mathbf{x}$ — naively, you just add $\lambda x_j$ to each
coordinate's gradient. But in Adam that penalty gradient then gets divided by $\sqrt{\hat
v_j}$ along with everything else, so coordinates with large gradients get *less* effective
weight decay than intended — the regularization is coupled to the adaptive scaling and
distorted. **AdamW** (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2017) *decouples* weight decay from the gradient
update: it applies the per-coordinate Adam step to the *loss* gradient only, then shrinks the
weights by a separate $\eta\lambda x_j$ term outside the $\sqrt{\hat v}$ rescaling,

$$
x_{j,\,k+1} = x_{j,k} - \eta\,\frac{\hat m_{j,k}}{\sqrt{\hat v_{j,k}}+\varepsilon}
              \;-\; \eta\,\lambda\,x_{j,k}.
$$

This small change measurably improves generalization and is now the default for training large
models. The lesson threads back to the *why*: adaptivity rescales *everything* it touches, so
anything you want applied uniformly — like weight decay — must be kept *outside* the rescaling.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Adam is the default — so why does anyone still use plain SGD or SGD-with-momentum?"* Three
reasons, and a strong answer hits all three. **(1) Generalization:** on data-rich problems
with careful tuning, SGD+momentum often reaches a *lower test* loss (@sec-ex-edge,
@sec-sgdm) — Adam optimizes the training objective more aggressively, which is not always what
you want. **(2) Convergence guarantees:** plain Adam can fail to converge on adversarial convex
problems (@sec-amsgrad-adamw); SGD's convergence theory ([OPTM-11]) is cleaner and AMSGrad was
invented to patch Adam's. **(3) Simplicity and memory:** Adam stores *two* extra
per-parameter buffers ($m$ and $v$) — for a billion-parameter model that is gigabytes of
optimizer state, where SGD+momentum needs only one buffer and plain SGD none. The honest
framing: Adam is the default because it is *fast and forgiving*, not because it is universally
*best* — and naming the regimes where SGD wins is the difference between repeating folklore
and understanding it.
:::

## A note on $\varepsilon$, and other quiet gotchas {#sec-eps}

A few small but real traps round out the practitioner picture. The constant $\varepsilon$ in
the denominator is not purely cosmetic: it caps the *maximum* effective step a coordinate can
take (when $\sqrt{\hat v_j}\to0$, the step approaches $\eta/\varepsilon$ rather than infinity),
so on problems where some gradients are genuinely tiny, a too-small $\varepsilon$ can let those
coordinates take wild steps — some practitioners deliberately raise $\varepsilon$ (to $10^{-4}$
or more) to tame this. The choice of $\beta_2$ trades responsiveness for stability: closer to
$1$ means a longer, steadier second-moment memory but slower reaction to a genuine change in
gradient scale. And **learning-rate warmup** — starting $\eta$ near zero and ramping it up over
the first few hundred or thousand steps — is now standard with Adam for large models, precisely
because even with bias correction the second-moment estimate is high-variance in the opening
steps, and a large early step on a noisy $\sqrt{\hat v}$ can destabilize training. Warmup,
schedules (cosine decay, step decay), and AdamW are the engineering layer wrapped around the
clean update of @eq-adam; the math is settled, the recipes are craft.

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You would never hand-code
@eq-adam in production. Every framework ships all three: `torch.optim.SGD` (with a `momentum`
argument), `torch.optim.Adagrad`, `torch.optim.RMSprop`, `torch.optim.Adam`, and
`torch.optim.AdamW`; the same names appear in `optax` (JAX), `keras.optimizers`, and
`tensorflow`. The defaults baked in are exactly the ones derived here —
$\beta_1=0.9,\beta_2=0.999,\varepsilon=10^{-8}$ for Adam — and the bias-correction step of
@sec-adam-bias is applied internally, which is why a fresh Adam run does not explode on step
one. What you now know that the API does not advertise: *why* there are two moment buffers,
*why* the denominator is a square root of an EMA, *why* the first few steps are
bias-corrected, *why* AdamW exists separately from Adam, and *why* the loss curve being lower
is not the same as the model being better. The library is the engine; this article is the
schematic.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[OPTM-11 *Stochastic Gradient Descent*]** (the noisy gradient, its variance,
and the noise floor that motivates per-coordinate care), carrying **[OPTM-08 *Momentum*]**
(the velocity / first-moment EMA) and **[OPTM-07 *Step Size and Convergence*]** (the condition
number $\kappa$ that anisotropy is all about). This article assembled them into the three
adaptive optimizers: **AdaGrad** (cumulative-sum scaling, with its monotone-decay flaw),
**RMSProp** (the EMA fix), and **Adam** (RMSProp + momentum + exact bias correction), plus the
honest debate (Adam's generalization gap, AMSGrad, AdamW).

This is the **terminal node of the descent spine.** From here:

- **The constrained-optimization arc — [OPTM-13 *Lagrange Multipliers*] onward** — turns from
  *how to descend* to *how to descend while honoring constraints*, the other half of the
  optimization story.
- **As a supply depot:** when **Classification**, **Unsupervised Learning**, and every
  deep-model treatment say "trained with Adam" or "fit by SGD with momentum," *this* is the
  article that says what those words mean — the per-coordinate adaptive step derived here is
  the literal engine of modern model training.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding papers.** J. Duchi, E. Hazan & Y. Singer, "Adaptive Subgradient Methods for
  Online Learning and Stochastic Optimization," *JMLR* 12 (2011) — AdaGrad and the regret
  argument of @sec-adagrad-regret. G. Hinton's Coursera *Neural Networks* lecture 6 (2012) —
  the original (unpublished) RMSProp slide. D. Kingma & J. Ba, "Adam: A Method for Stochastic
  Optimization," *ICLR* (2015) — Adam, the bias-correction derivation of @sec-adam-bias, and
  the moment-estimation framing.
- **The honest debate.** S. Reddi, S. Kale & S. Kumar, "On the Convergence of Adam and
  Beyond," *ICLR* (2018) — the non-convergence counterexample and AMSGrad. A. Wilson et al.,
  "The Marginal Value of Adaptive Gradient Methods in Machine Learning," *NeurIPS* (2017) — the
  empirical case that tuned SGD generalizes better (@sec-ex-edge). I. Loshchilov & F. Hutter,
  "Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization," *ICLR* (2019) — AdamW.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do these interact with
  learning-rate schedules and warmup?* — engineering craft sketched in @sec-eps, with a deep
  literature of its own. *What is the second moment really estimating — diagonal curvature?* —
  there is a precise connection to a diagonal approximation of the Hessian / Fisher information
  that ties adaptivity back to the second-order methods of [OPTM-09]; that link is the natural
  rabbit hole. *Do adaptive methods change the implicit regularization of SGD?* — the
  flat-minima / generalization question raised in [OPTM-11]'s non-convex discussion, here sharpened by the
  Adam-vs-SGD gap, remains an active research frontier.
